findings.title: Leaked production credentials in git history (Neon DB + Stack Auth)
findings.description: Commit f4d507f added backend/.env with plaintext Neon database URL (including password [REDACTED]), Stack Auth secret server key ([REDACTED]), and Stack Auth publishable client key ([REDACTED]). Removed ~2 hours later in commit 25546b2. Credentials have been rotated/services decommissioned. Git history scrubbed.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Clone/fork the repository. 2. Run git log -p --all -- backend/.env. 3. Extract the Neon DB connection string. 4. Connect directly to the database with psql. 5. Read/modify all user data, sessions, articles. 6. Use Stack Auth keys to impersonate auth provider.
findings.impact: Full database access if credentials not rotated. User PII exposure (emails, profiles, reading behavior). Session token theft enabling account takeover.
findings.recommendation: 1. Rotate ALL leaked credentials immediately (Neon DB password, Stack Auth keys). 2. Use git filter-repo or BFG Repo-Cleaner to purge backend/.env from history. 3. Force-push cleaned history. 4. Check Neon and Stack Auth audit logs for unauthorized access.
findings.playbook: COMPLETED: Services decommissioned. Git history scrubbed with git-filter-repo. Checkpoint refs cleaned.

findings.title: Session tokens are deterministic and forgeable
findings.description: Session tokens generated as SHA256(user_id:provider_user_id:unix_timestamp) contain zero bits of randomness. All three inputs are knowable or guessable. Brute-forcing the timestamp (86,400 candidates per day) reconstructs the exact token.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Obtain target user's UUID (exposed in API responses or guessable). 2. Obtain their Google/Apple sub ID (stable, sometimes public). 3. Guess approximate login time (within a day = 86,400 SHA256 hashes = milliseconds). 4. Compute SHA256 for each timestamp candidate. 5. Use the forged token as Bearer header to impersonate the user.
findings.impact: Account takeover for any user. Attacker gains full access to victim's feed, chat history, preferences, and reading behavior.
findings.recommendation: Replace with: import secrets; session_token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32). Apply to both /auth/google (line 691) and /auth/apple (line 733).

findings.title: Apple JWT audience verification disabled
findings.description: jwt.decode() called with options={'verify_aud': False} and no issuer parameter. A valid Apple identity token issued for ANY application can authenticate against this backend. The comment 'Apple doesn't always include aud' is incorrect -- Apple Sign In tokens always include aud.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker runs their own app with Sign in with Apple. 2. Victim signs into attacker's app, generating a valid Apple-signed JWT. 3. Attacker presents victim's token to /auth/apple on Daily's backend. 4. Backend accepts it (aud not checked), creates session for victim's Apple sub ID. 5. Attacker now has an authenticated session as the victim.
findings.impact: Account takeover via cross-application token reuse. Any app developer using Sign in with Apple can impersonate users of this app.
findings.recommendation: Enable audience and issuer verification: jwt.decode(identity_token, key=public_key, algorithms=['RS256'], audience='com.Muhammad.Daily', issuer='https://appleid.apple.com')

findings.title: SSRF via AI-suggested feed URLs fetched without network restrictions
findings.description: OpenAI generates full RSS feed URLs in _ai_suggest_feeds(). These URLs are fetched by the server in _validate_candidate_sources() with httpx.AsyncClient(follow_redirects=True) and zero URL validation. No scheme enforcement, no private IP blocklist, no metadata endpoint protection.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Craft user interests that steer the LLM to suggest a specific URL (indirect prompt injection). 2. LLM returns http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ as a 'feed URL'. 3. Server fetches the URL, reads cloud metadata. 4. Response content processed by feedparser and stored. On Fly.io: access instance metadata, environment variables, internal services.
findings.impact: Cloud metadata exposure (API keys, instance credentials). Internal service port scanning. Potential lateral movement within Fly.io infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Add URL validation in _build_ai_candidates(): enforce https:// only, resolve hostname and reject RFC1918/link-local/loopback IPs (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 127.x, 169.254.x), block metadata hostnames.

findings.title: No per-user rate limiting on LLM chat endpoints (financial risk)
findings.description: Chat endpoints (/chat/threads/{id}/messages/stream, /chat, /chat/interests) have zero per-user rate limiting. Each message triggers 1-3 sequential OpenAI API calls (routing + planning + streaming). No global budget cap or circuit breaker exists.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Authenticate as any user. 2. Script rapid-fire messages to /chat/threads/{id}/messages/stream. 3. Each message triggers up to 3 OpenAI API calls. 4. 1000 messages/hour x 3 calls x ~500 tokens = significant API spend. 5. No circuit breaker stops the bleeding.
findings.impact: Unbounded OpenAI API costs. A single malicious user could run up hundreds of dollars in API fees overnight. The /sources/discover endpoint has a 5-minute cooldown showing awareness of this pattern, but chat endpoints lack it.
findings.recommendation: Add per-user rate limiting (e.g., 30 messages/hour) via slowapi or middleware. Add a global daily spend cap that disables non-critical LLM calls when threshold reached.

findings.title: pyjwt==2.9.0 vulnerable to CVE-2026-32597 (crit header bypass)
findings.description: pyjwt 2.9.0 does not validate the 'crit' (Critical) Header Parameter per RFC 7515 Section 4.1.11. Tokens with unknown critical extensions are accepted instead of rejected. Fix available in pyjwt>=2.12.0.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker crafts a JWT with a 'crit' header containing unknown extensions. 2. pyjwt accepts the token without checking the critical extensions. 3. Security policies encoded via critical headers are bypassed.
findings.impact: JWT validation bypass in mixed-library deployments. Combined with Finding #3 (disabled aud verification), further weakens token validation.
findings.recommendation: Upgrade: pip install pyjwt>=2.12.0 and update requirements.txt.

findings.title: Production Dockerfile runs as root
findings.description: The production Dockerfile has no USER directive. Container processes run as root. Any RCE vulnerability gives the attacker root-level access inside the container.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Exploit any code execution vulnerability (SSRF + deserialization, prompt injection leading to code exec, dependency CVE). 2. Attacker lands in container as root. 3. Read /proc/1/environ for all environment variables (DATABASE_URL, OPENAI_API_KEY). 4. Mount volumes, access host filesystem if Docker socket exposed.
findings.impact: Root container access. Environment variable extraction (DB credentials, API keys). Potential container escape depending on runtime configuration.
findings.recommendation: Add to Dockerfile: RUN useradd --create-home appuser && USER appuser. Place after pip install, before CMD.

findings.title: Verbose error messages leak internal details to clients
findings.description: Multiple endpoints return detail=f'Internal server error: {str(e)}' in HTTP 500 responses. Raw Python exception strings can leak database connection details, SQL errors, file paths, and library-specific internals. Inconsistent pattern -- some endpoints use generic messages, others leak.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Send malformed requests to trigger errors. 2. Read the error detail field in 500 responses. 3. Extract database type, table names, file paths, library versions. 4. Use information for targeted attacks (SQL injection payloads, path traversal, CVE exploitation).
findings.impact: Information disclosure aiding attacker reconnaissance. Database schema exposure. Internal file path disclosure.
findings.recommendation: Replace all str(e) in HTTPException detail with generic messages. Log full exceptions server-side with logging.exception().

findings.title: Sign-out does not invalidate server-side sessions
findings.description: AuthService.signOut() only deletes the token from the local Keychain. No backend call invalidates the session. Compromised tokens remain valid for their full 30-day lifetime. No 'revoke all sessions' endpoint exists.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker steals a session token (device theft, network intercept, token prediction via Finding #2). 2. Victim signs out on their device. 3. Stolen token still works for up to 30 days. 4. Victim has no way to invalidate the compromised session.
findings.impact: Stolen tokens cannot be revoked. User has no recourse after compromise except waiting 30 days for expiry.
findings.recommendation: Add DELETE /auth/session endpoint that removes the session row from DB. Call it from iOS signOut(). Add DELETE /auth/sessions/all for 'sign out everywhere'.
